one_line_takeaway: ProAgentBench is a real-world (not LLM-synthesized), privacy-filtered, 28,528-event/500+-hour dataset and When-to-Assist/How-to-Assist evaluation framework for proactive AI agents; it shows real human interaction is measurably burstier than LLM-simulated timing, that knowledge-graph-based long-term memory materially improves both tasks, and that fine-tuning on real data beats fine-tuning on equal-sized synthetic data across every metric tested -- but this is an HCI/agent-evaluation paper, not an AI-security paper.
key_finding: (1) Real human interaction data is meaningfully burstier than LLM-simulated interaction timing: human IETs are power-law distributed (alpha=1.50, log-likelihood ratio=2951.48, p=7.83e-100 favoring power-law) with burstiness B=0.787, while LLM-simulated interaction timing under the same candidate time points is closer to exponential (log-likelihood ratio=-59.36, p=8.37e-7 favoring exponential) with B=0.166 -- i.e., LLMs do not naturally reproduce human bursty timing even given realistic candidate timestamps. (2) On the base prompted-baseline comparison (Table 2), Deepseek-V3.2 zero-shot gets the best When-to-Assist accuracy (64.4%), Qwen3-VL-Plus zero-shot gets the best Intention Accuracy (37.1%), and all models' Semantic Similarity is low and tightly clustered (0.275-0.305), which the authors read as evidence that even frontier LLMs struggle to generate assistance content matching what users actually needed. CoT prompting helps larger/closed models but can hurt smaller open models (e.g., Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct CoT accuracy drops to 41.0% vs 51.7% zero-shot). (3) Long-term memory integration helps substantially: a Knowledge-Graph memory method raises Accuracy from 0.537 to 0.601 (+11.8%), Intention Accuracy from 0.312 to 0.396 (+26.9%), and F1 from 0.675 to 0.716 (+6.1%) over the zero-shot baseline; RAG gives smaller gains (Accuracy 0.550, +2.4%; Intention Accuracy 0.332, +6.3%; F1 0.681, +0.8%). (4) Fine-tuning on real-world data beats fine-tuning on equal-sized synthetic data across every metric tested: for LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct, SFT on real data reaches 74.0% Accuracy / 78.5% F1 / 42.1% Intention Accuracy / 0.385 Semantic Similarity vs. SFT on synthetic data at 62.1% / 70.2% / 34.8% / 0.312 (zero-shot baseline 57.3% / 66.7% / 32.3% / 0.275); the same real->synthetic gap holds for Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct (SFT real: 63.5%/72.4%/41.8%/0.358 vs SFT synthetic: 54.8%/67.8%/36.2%/0.295) and for LoRA variants of both models.
core_contribution: The paper introduces ProAgentBench, a benchmark for evaluating LLM/VLM-based 'proactive' AI agents (agents that decide when to intervene and what to offer without explicit user prompts) in real-world computer-use workflows. Three claimed contributions: (1) a hierarchical 'When to Assist' (binary timing classification) + 'How to Assist' (assistance-content generation) task decomposition; (2) a privacy-filtered dataset of 28,528 events (7,222 LLM-related, ~25.3%) collected from 500+ hours of real student users' screen/app-usage logs via a tool called LifeTrace, capturing pre-assistance behavioral context and bursty inter-event timing (burstiness B=0.787, power-law IET exponent alpha=1.50) that the authors show LLM-synthesized data (from the Sun et al. 2025 method) fails to reproduce (B=0.166, exponential-fit-favored); and (3) baseline experiments across six LLMs/VLMs, an ablation on historical-context length, a comparison of memory strategies (RAG, Knowledge Graph, Clustering), and a real-vs-synthetic-data fine-tuning study.
threat_model: Not applicable. This is not a security paper and does not define an attacker, adversary, or threat model. The 'Privacy Protection' section (Sec. 5.2) and the Ethics Statement (Sec. 8.2) describe defensive data-collection hygiene for the authors' own human-subjects study (VLM-based PII screening, human-in-the-loop review, rule-based filtering, restricted access under data-use agreements) rather than a security contribution, attack, or defense evaluated as part of the paper's research question. Treating this as an AI-security paper would be forcing a frame the paper does not adopt.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations (Sec. 8.1): (1) participant-selection bias -- volunteers are students from a limited set of professions/tech stacks/regions/languages, so OS and application-ecosystem differences may hurt generalization to broader populations; (2) 1Hz sampling can miss very short interactions, and unstable/dynamic window titles can cause annotation errors; (3) the aggressive privacy-filtering pipeline, while necessary for ethical release, may systematically exclude sensitive-content interactions, biasing the released dataset toward less privacy-sensitive workflows. Reviewer-observed caveats: the paper is a preprint (Feb 2026, cs.HC) released with an anonymized code/data link (anonymous.4open.science), so the dataset was not independently verifiable at review time; the real-vs-synthetic fine-tuning comparison (RQ3) uses a small paired sample (741 real vs 741 synthetic instances) on only 2 of the 6 evaluated open-source models; and best zero-shot timing accuracy across all six evaluated LLMs/VLMs tops out at 64.4%, with How-to-Assist intention accuracy topping out at 37.1% and semantic similarity never exceeding 0.305 -- i.e. the benchmark itself shows current LLMs are still far from reliable at the proactive-assistance task the paper defines.

datasets_used: ProAgentBench (own dataset): 28,528 total events, 7,222 LLM-related (~25.3%), 500+ hours of continuous real user sessions, collected via LifeTrace at 1Hz screenshot + app-metadata logging
Synthetic comparison data generated following the Sun et al. (2025) method, matched in size to the real-world fine-tuning subset (741 instances) for RQ3
quantitative_results: Human IET: power-law exponent alpha=1.50, log-likelihood ratio=2951.48 vs exponential (p=7.83e-100), burstiness B=0.787
LLM-simulated IET: log-likelihood ratio=-59.36 favoring exponential (p=8.37e-7), burstiness B=0.166
Zero-shot When-to-Assist best: Deepseek-V3.2 Accuracy 64.4% (Precision 60.8%, Recall 81.1%, F1 69.5%)
Zero-shot How-to-Assist best Intention Accuracy: Qwen3-VL-Plus 37.1% (Semantic Similarity 0.286)
Zero-shot Semantic Similarity range across all 6 models: 0.275-0.286 (and CoT variants up to 0.305)
Knowledge-Graph memory vs zero-shot: Accuracy 0.537->0.601 (+11.8%), Intention Accuracy 0.312->0.396 (+26.9%), F1 0.675->0.716 (+6.1%)
RAG memory vs zero-shot: Accuracy +2.4% to 0.550, Intention Accuracy +6.3% to 0.332, F1 +0.8% to 0.681
baselines_compared: Models: GPT-4o-mini, Qwen3-Max, Deepseek-V3.2, Qwen3-VL-Plus (closed-source); LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct, Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct (open-source)
Memory-integration methods: RAG, Knowledge Graph, Clustering (PersonaX-inspired)

Prior proactive-agent datasets compared in Table 1 (cited, not the paper's own results): Lu et al. 2024 ProactiveBench (6,790 events, mixed real/synthetic), Sun et al. 2025 (6,563 events, mixed), Yang et al. 2025a ContextAgent (1,000 events, mixed), Yang et al. 2025b FingerTip 20K (21,437 events, real), Liu et al. 2025b ProactiveEval (328 environments, mixed)
